sections.question: Two corporations, A and B, jointly retain a lobbyist. Corporation A maintains an office for the lobbyist and makes all payments for salary and office maintenance. Corporation B reimburses Corporation A for one half of total payments and office costs. The specific questions are: (1) Does the prohibition detailed in Regulation §18619 apply to Corporation A? (2) Does it apply to Corporation B? (3) How should the lobbyist report compensation? (4) How should contributions/gifts be reported? (5) How should total payments be reported?
sections.conclusion: Corporation A is not a 'firm, corporation or other business entity which is retained for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence legislative or administrative action' as described in the Commission's regulation defining lobbyist under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18619. Therefore, the prohibitions on gifts and contributions contained in Government Code §86202 will not be applicable to the assets of Corporation A if it is an owner of 10% or more of the common stock of Corporation B. The prohibitions on gifts and contributions will apply to the assets of Corporation B in the same manner as for Corporation A. The lobbyist should continue to report on their individual capacity as well as for each corporation.
sections.question_synthetic: When two nonprofit corporations jointly retain a lobbyist with one corporation handling all payments and the other reimbursing for half the costs, which entity is subject to the lobbyist disclosure and gift/contribution prohibitions under the Political Reform Act, and how should compensation and payments be reported?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Corporation A is not subject to the lobbyist definition and gift/contribution prohibitions under the regulation if it owns 10% or more of Corporation B's stock. Corporation B remains subject to the same prohibitions as Corporation A would be, and the lobbyist must report compensation and expenses from each corporation individually.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the original memo request and FPPC response. The document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and missing/garbled text throughout, particularly in the conclusion section. Some specific answers to questions 3, 4, and 5 are partially illegible. The core legal conclusion regarding Corporation A's status is clear, but supporting details are compromised.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Two corporations, A and B, jointly retain a lobbyist. Corporation A maintains an office for the lobbyist and makes all payments for salary and office maintenance. Corporation B reimburses Corporation A for one half of total payments and office costs. The specific questions are: (1) Does the prohibition detailed in Regulation §18619 apply to Corporation A? (2) Does it apply to Corporation B? (3) How should the lobbyist report compensation? (4) How should contributions/gifts be reported? (5) How should total payments be reported?

CONCLUSION: Corporation A is not a 'firm, corporation or other business entity which is retained for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence legislative or administrative action' as described in the Commission's regulation defining lobbyist under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18619. Therefore, the prohibitions on gifts and contributions contained in Government Code §86202 will not be applicable to the assets of Corporation A if it is an owner of 10% or more of the common stock of Corporation B. The prohibitions on gifts and contributions will apply to the assets of Corporation B in the same manner as for Corporation A. The lobbyist should continue to report on their individual capacity as well as for each corporation.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses the application of political reform lobbyist disclosure and gift/contribution prohibitions when two nonprofit corporations jointly retain a lobbyist with cost-sharing arrangements. The FPPC concludes that Corporation A is not a retained lobbying firm under the regulation and clarifies reporting requirements for both entities.
